key_insight: Adjoint optimization and PINNs are most effective when matched to the unknown’s representation: grid-based fields tend to favor adjoints, while neural representations favor PINNs.
engineering_relevance: For PDE-constrained inverse problems in mechanics and fluids, the choice of solver can materially affect accuracy, cost, and practicality depending on whether the target is a field, closure term, or constitutive parameter.
method: Use a common inverse-problem formulation with identical PDEs, observations, regularization, optimizer, and precision.
Compare discrete adjoint optimization against PINNs on unsteady Burgers, noisy Darcy, 3D Allen--Cahn, and unsteady Navier--Stokes inversion tasks.
Test a PINN-warm-started adjoint workflow to combine fast approximate recovery with high-accuracy refinement.
evidence_signal: The comparison is benchmarked on four PDE inverse problems and evaluates reconstruction quality and computational cost under matched settings.
watch_item: Results depend strongly on how the unknown is parameterized, so conclusions may not transfer directly if the discretization, neural representation, or training setup changes.
paper_title: Adjoint Method versus Physics-Informed Neural Networks in PDE-Constrained Inverse Problems
